findings.description: Full card number and CVV are written to application logs at INFO level, a PCI DSS violation since CVV must never be persisted anywhere after authorization.
charge_order only checks that the order exists, never its status, so an already-paid order can be charged again, and concurrent calls can race to double-charge before either commits.
The UPDATE marks the order paid but never sets paid_at, yet monthly_revenue_report filters on strftime of paid_at, so charged orders silently never appear in revenue reports.
refund() only flips the DB status to 'refunded' and never calls the payment gateway (which exposes no refund API at all), so the customer's money is never actually returned.
Revenue is computed as raw unit_price*quantity, ignoring the discount_pct (and tax) that determined the actual charged amount, so reported revenue diverges from money collected.
Transaction IDs derive from the salted built-in hash(), so they are not deterministic across processes despite the comment and they collide for repeat charges with the same last-4 and amount.
Monetary values flow through binary floats (unit_price, TAX_RATE, subtotals), inviting cumulative rounding errors where integer cents or Decimal should be used.
discount_pct is never validated, so negative values silently inflate the price and values over 100 produce negative order totals.
refund's status check and UPDATE are not atomic (no AND status='paid' guard in the UPDATE and no transaction), so concurrent calls can both pass the check and double-refund.
If the gateway charge succeeds but the subsequent UPDATE/commit fails or the process crashes, the customer is charged with no 'paid' record, and no idempotency key exists for safe retry.
monthly_revenue_report issues one order_items query per order inside a loop (N+1) instead of a single JOIN/GROUP BY aggregate.
Wrapping paid_at in strftime() makes the date predicate non-sargable, forcing a full table scan and defeating any index on paid_at.
The report rounds each line item to cents independently while the charge path rounds once at the order total, so the two can disagree by pennies per order.
Python round() uses banker's rounding (half-to-even) for the cents conversion, which can differ by one cent from the half-up rounding conventionally expected in billing.
Orders whose total is zero (e.g., 100% discount or free items) can never be marked paid because the gateway rejects non-positive amounts.
Filtering on current status='paid' means a later refund retroactively removes the order from historical monthly revenue, making past reports unstable.
Raw PAN and CVV are passed through application code with no tokenization, placing this entire layer inside PCI DSS scope.
year/month inputs are unvalidated, so impossible values like month=13 silently return an all-zero report instead of raising an error.
SELECT * fetches all order columns when only discount_pct is actually used, over-fetching and staying fragile against schema changes.
The cvv parameter is accepted but completely unused by the stub charge implementation.
refund() returns a bare False for both 'order not found' and 'order not in paid state', leaving callers unable to distinguish the cases.
TAX_RATE is a hard-coded single-jurisdiction constant baked into the module rather than being configurable.
